summary: Cantrill dismantles NetApp engineer Mike Eisler's defense of SPEC SFS benchmark's lack of pricing disclosure, arguing that claims about opaque pricing benefiting customers and component costs falling too fast to track are absurd rationalizations. He coins the term 'Moore's Outlaws' to describe enterprise storage vendors who use outdated components while claiming technology curves justify their pricing opacity. The post argues that pricing disclosure is essential for benchmark credibility and that storage vendors' configurations don't reflect what customers actually purchase.
key_points: SPEC SFS benchmark lacks pricing disclosure, unlike TPC and SPC benchmarks
NetApp's defense of opaque pricing as benefiting customers is logically absurd
Enterprise storage vendors use outdated components while invoking Moore's Law to justify pricing opacity
Drive latency hasn't improved due to physical constraints (15K RPM ceiling), undermining technology curve arguments
Benchmark configurations don't correspond to systems customers actually deploy
money_quotes: Someone should let the buyers know how great they have it – those silly buyers don't seem to realize that the endless haggling over software licensing and support contracts is for them!

The single-core Mobile Celeron that NetApp currently ships in their FAS2020 and FAS2050 – a CPU with a criminally small 256K of L2 cache – is best described as a Moore's Outlaw: a CPU that, even when it first shipped six years ago, was off the curve.
It's no wonder that NetApp sees plummeting component costs when they're able to source their CPUs by dumpster diving.
We all understand that prices reflect a moment in time, and if natural inflation does not dissuade us from price disclosures, nor should any technology-induced deflation.
SPEC SFS needs pricing disclosures. TPC has them, SPC has them – and SFS needs them if the benchmark has any aspiration to enduring relevance.
key_insight: Enterprise storage vendors invoke technology improvement curves to justify pricing opacity while simultaneously shipping outdated components that violate those very curves - a hypocrisy that benchmark pricing disclosures would expose.
